sentences.text: My name is Moshe Morris Perlmutter.
I was born in 1929, May 3, 1929.
Yeah, that's right.
I'm today, 66.
I will be 67 in May.
A small station town, Malynsk, in Ukraine.
Well, if I start from my life, I was born to a family in which my father was married before, and he had three children.
My mother was married before, and she had two children.
And then between them, they had two sons, which I'm one of them.
My other brothers and sister left for Israel in the '30s.
My two brothers, on my mother's side, were taken to the Russian army.
And they were in Russia all through.
Me and my brother and my mother remained in Ukraine for a time.
And if I go back, I'll be starting when I started school in 1936-- when I started school, I went to school in [NON-ENGLISH], It was very big.
The teacher never called me by my name, called me only Jew-- Jew to the blackboard, Jew any question, or anything like that.
Incidents that he rose guys to beat me up.
And one time, they told me not to come to school, we will beat you up.
And on the way to school, I picked up a-- a little branch from a tree.
And I put it in my pocket, and I went.
And there was a few guys who met me.
And they were, Jew, you shouldn't go to school.
And one of them jumped me.
And then I took the branch-- I hit him over the face.
And when it really hurt him, he started crying.
And that was the end of that.
And I went to school.
And they gave me a little bit more respect for that, that I hit back.
At the time, I was seven years old.
At home, we were like an ordinary Jewish orthodox family, which now we call Orthodox.
We didn't know about any other different ways, because we were Jewish.
My father had a bakery.
And we lived in that small town only for one reason-- my father was in the war of 1903 with the Japanese and the Russians.
In 1915, he was in the Russian War again.
He was wounded in the feet.
And then they gave him that piece of land there.
And that's where he built a house there.
And he had a bakery.
He was a baker.
And he was baking bread.
For Passover, for instance, we used to clean out a few days before.
And we used to bake matzah.
The people used to come in, and they used to roll the matzah-- I was small.
And I still remember when they used to make the matzah.
In our house, we always used to pray.
There was no synagogue in our village.
And I think there were about 60 to 70 Jewish people living in that town.
Out of a population of probably about 300 to 400.
I don't know exactly.
I don't remember now.
The only thing-- that was a very popular place, because it was a station.
And a lot of people used to travel to different parts from that country.
And a lot of Jewish people stayed overnight in our place, or different places.
Some they paid, some didn't.
And we always had people for the Shabbos if they couldn't go back home.
We always had for the holidays-- we used to-- from the villages, they used to come to pray.
In the later years, in the '30s, they decided to build a synagogue.
And my father gave a piece of his land and the neighbor.
And they were going to build a synagogue on their property.
And I remember they already bought some wood, and everything else.
But in 1938, '39-- I don't remember-- the anti-Semitism was very big.
We couldn't sell the stuff anymore.
And they used to put labels on the windows-- anybody who buys from a Jew is a pig, with pigs and everything else.
And all kind of harassment was all the time going on.
To me, actually, it wasn't.
Because as a boy, we only kept between us.
The only time we had to do with any of the Gentile boys-- friends or anything, make friends.
We never made.
We kept between the Jewish children.
And--
No, no, no.
My father had non-Jewish customers in the bakery.
Besides, he was not only the baker, he was a glass cutter.
He had a small, little diamond.
And he used to cut glass.
And anybody who had a broken window or anything like that, they used to bring it to him, even from the villages.
And a lot of times, I used to go with him.
We used to have a horse and buggy.
And we used to drive out to the villages.
And if they didn't have what to pay, and he said-- my father used to say to them, whenever you have something in the field, and everything else, you bring it to me.
They used to bring potatoes.
And they used to bring corn, and they used to bring different things for-- to pay my father for that.
So there was no problem.
But actually, later on-- like I said, in '38, we had more, because we had a competition.
There was a Polish guy who opened up a bakery.
And he didn't want to buy from us.
And we had a better bread than he had.
So they-- we used to supply to a lot of non-Jewish people.
They liked my father.
And my father's name was a very good known name in the village.
Describe the layout of your house.
Where was the bakery?
Where did you live?
No, no.
It was in one house together.
The bakery was just a section, different from the house.
But we lived just in the other side.
We had a little store, which we used to sell.
Sometimes my mother used to bake up a cake and sell.
And we used to have all kinds of different things in the store, too.
I don't know, three bedrooms, or something like that.
And there was a big dining room and a kitchen, which we had.

The dining room I remember big because it was a long table, and we always had-- on a Pesach, we had about 12, 15 people because a lot of them in the war didn't make it to the other town who came for Passover to us.
And they wanted a Seder.
Some people didn't have what to eat-- they came.
And we were not bad-off.
We were not rich, but we were not one of the poor people.
Just Yiddish.
Well, I spoke Polish, and I spoke Ukrainian.
But I spoke good Polish because at that time, I was going to school for Polish.
We only learned Polish.
But mostly I spoke a little bit of Ukrainian, too.
Oh, my extended family was a very big family-- not in the town.
My grandfather had five children-- three daughters and three sons, and not young ones, like I said.
Because I never remembered my grandfather.
He lived to 104, and I never remembered him.
And the only one was-- survived, what she had a son in Canada, which I never knew.
And the other five was perished.
Now, they had many children, many grandchildren.
On my mother's side, I had-- well, they had 11 children.
But five of them-- no, yeah, five of them were here, in the States, and in Canada.
And six of them perished.
Also, my mother was the youngest of them all.
They were all married.
They had grandchildren, which I knew some of them which I met in the ghetto afterwards.
Then only one cousin survived.
He died in Israel, later.
Well, the Passover was a Seder, which my father had to have a [NON-ENGLISH] made.
Everybody had a-- a different glass, a different to drink from.
Everybody had a different design.
One incident, like I say, when I was small that I remember-- you know, like they put a glass for a [NON-ENGLISH], the glass for the angel.
And they opened the door.
And somebody shook the table.
And he says, you see, he drank it from it.
Yes, every day my father was praying-- for me, too, to [NON-ENGLISH], or you know, and all that, I had to say, pretty well every day.
Yes, and we all prayed most of the time.
Oh, in a small town in the summertime, we used to make our own toys.
There were no toys to be bought.
And we made our own wagons.
We handmade all the different things.
We ran around.
We were--between the Jewish families, we had lots of children my age.
In my class, there were three of us.
And there were others that we followed around, together.
And in wintertime, we were on the sleds, and we were making snowmen and all that.
Not-- all on our own.
We never got any different things.
All the young children, of course, on the holidays, we were celebrating Hanukkah or Purim, or anything like that.
It was very nice.
Well, maybe Hanukkah because of Hanukkah gelt.
You got some money.
No, we used to dress up.
Yeah.
And that's all.
In that period, I don't know.
It went through so fast till 1939.
You know, in 1939, the-- the Russians came in over to us.
So already, the religion died off a lot.
But till then, every holiday, we-- we celebrated, we prepared.
We always made different chickens, and different things.
With my mother, used to prepare and cook and  all that.
She was like-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] see, I remember my mother of later, because she was always sick.
And I had to take care of her all the time.
But in the early days, she was always busy to make a living, to cook or bake something for the store to sell, or anything like that.
Nothing special that I would remember about my mother.
My father-- I loved my father very much.
But he was a busy man.
And he was a sick man.
He had asthma.
And see, there was a big difference in the age between my mother and father.
I think about 15 years.
Because my mother was left over with two sons.
Which her first son had died from typhus.
And they got married.
And of course, like I said before, they had the two sons.
And they had to provide for them.
And life was a quiet life in a small village.
The only thing we used to go out in the evening to go to see the train coming in, who was coming off, who was going away.
And the only thing is if anybody left for Israel, there was a big party in town.
Everybody was invited.
Oh, it was in our house or somebody else's house.
And most of the people went to Israel, to Palestine those days.
And some left to the United States.
Some left for Argentina.
In 19-- I think-- '38-- my brothers wanted us to come to Israel.
And my father says, no, I won't go anywhere.
I'll stay here till I-- for the rest of my life.
I don't want depend on children.
And that's why.
My mother actually did want to go.
That I remember.
But it-- cause it never came through.
Yeah.
No, no, not in those days.
Before, there was kibbutzim in our place.
Because, why I say that  it's not was in my time.
It was in the early '30s, which there were kibbutzim.
They were working on the-- in the field, and in the forest.
And that's how my two brothers and sister left with a kibbutzim to Israel.
So there was a lot of [NON-ENGLISH], which they were working in our place.
But in my time, there wasn't any more.
No, there weren't any organization of kibbutzim.
Before then, yes, because that's when my brother-- they used to talk about the way they were working.
Yeah, and this.
They were talking quite a lot, because what it is-- but not in '38.
In '38, yes, we heard about it, but didn't know anything.
The only time we really started to get frightened was in 1939.
In 1939, there was a lot-- a lot-- I would say a lot, maybe-- Jewish people from all over were running away.
And they came through our town.
I met people from Germany that said the Germans were killing and they were taking everything away and Jews were dying from hunger and all kinds-- and on their way to Russia.
So in 1939, we already felt.
But we, in 1939, were better off than before, because the Russians gave us better freedom from anti-Semites than before.
The only thing was my father was like a capitalist there.
So he went to them, and he said, you can have the bakery.
They really appreciated that he gave away the bakery.
So  they said, because you gave the bakery, you'll be the manager.
He says, OK, I'll run it, you supply the flour and whatever you need, and we'll make.
But he was not a good manager for them.
From-- from each bag of flour, you had to give out so much-- so much bread.
And he couldn't do that, because he was baking out a good loaf of bread, and everything else.
And they had to have a bread that has lots of water in it, and it weighs a lot.
So he says, if that case, I'm too sick to run it.
You run it on your own.
So they took over, and they ran it on their own.
They took it over in the house.
When did the Russians come in?
That's what I'm saying.
In 1939, the Russians came in, they were there for two years.
At that time, even when the Russians came in, I say, was better for me.
In school, the teacher was still there that used to call me Jew.
He came to me and asked me-- and now almost begged me-- that I should not report him that he called me Jew.
Because if I would have maybe gone to the Russians, and-- that he is like that and everything else-- so I says, OK, I won't say nothing.
Of course, which I did not report him.
I didn't say nothing.
And actually, it turned out better that way, because two years later, the Russians were gone.
And the anti-Semitism at that time was huge than ever.
But the-- in time of the Russian, of course, I didn't learn any more Polish.
I was learning Russian and Ukrainian.
So we changed the languages.
And everything was against Polish.
Anybody was-- there was in-- any very rich Polish people, they took away to Siberia from our place, though.
Because it was a very rich district.
And for the Russians, we said that Jews will become a mensches, you know.
All of a sudden, we were people.
And me and my brothers were-- of course, they were taken away.
And one was taken away right away into the Russian army, and the second one went when they were going back.
They asked us if we wanted to go with them.
My father said-- that I remember-- he said, a war is only with the armies.
They don't touch civilians.
In that way, he was wrong.
Yes.
In 1941, the Germans came in.
The law enacted-- is only-- the only thing is, they didn't like to be religious.
You know, they just-- they made all kinds of funny things that you could say.
They said, you know, in a class with a small audience, or anything like that-- God, give us candy.
Of course, there was nothing coming around.
Stalin gave us candy.
There was candy flying all over the place-- so which they tried to convert to Stalinism and all that.
But for the Jewish people, they didn't bother if anybody was still praying, and everything else, in different ways.
But the younger people, which were going away from it-- you know, because they were afraid, or anything like that.
But for the Jewish people-- for the Jewish part, it was a lot better that I saw.
We were, like I said, people.
Yes, yes.
The daily prayers continued.
Like I said, just the older one of the 20, 25-- those-- those people-- there weren't that many in the town, anyway, except my two brothers and others.
Because most of those ages, they left off for Israel, or for any other part of the world.
Two years.
My brother and I would never get along.
He was a very sick boy.
And I was the strong one.
My mother used to pay him to eat.
And I said, I eat for nothing.
And he used to get paid-- because for one reason-- he was a weak boy.
And every time stubborn, and everything else.
So-- and if I stood up for him, he didn't like it.
And he was always on his own, always running away, always different.
I was running away a different way, but he was the-- so, my brother, we-- which I had only one real brother-- didn't get along.
No.
I was running away from different things.
No, I was responsible in the home.
It had to be done something, I swept up and I cleaned up.
He wouldn't do that.
Oh, no.
He wouldn't listen to Mother, to do anything.
And all different things I used to do.
I used to do.
OK, this is going to be a little bit later, then after the war, which I would say, I was the shechit at home.
My mother was kosher-- I used to take the chicken by the neck, and with a blade, and she used to say a prayer.
Cut it one time only-- my mother would standing.
And I used to kill the chicken.
My mother made it kosher.
And that's how we eat.
Before the war, no, because before the war, we had a shechit-- but after that, I did it all the time.
And we had a chicken already at that time.
The Jewish ritual, well, every Jew had a trade most of the time.
There was a tailor, there was a butcher, and we actually wound up having a doctor in that small place like that.
Came-- Dr.
[NON-ENGLISH].
And, of course, he was killed, one of the first-- the first, the Germans killed him.
And all the other Jewish people had different trades and different things, and we used to work with one another.
And we used to go to different villages or bring stuff or take stuff and all that.
And a lot of them were workers, just plain workers working.
We had a mill.
We had a lumber mill.
And in the same lumber mill was a flour mill.
The pre-war period?
Not very much, no.
It was just normal life going through is all.
That's about it.
Well, my life started to change at the time when the Germans came in.
The first thing when the Germans came in, the first front like came in, they were very nice people.
They lived in our house.
They moved in, they took in the house, and everything else.
The Germans taught me how to shoot a gun, a rifle.
It just hit me by the shoulder very hard, and everything else.
He showed me how to take it apart, put it together.
And then he said-- because we could commute-- OK, it's German-Jewish.
But it's not the same-- but we commute.
They understood us.
We understood them.
But the German said to me-- he said to my father, not to me, but I was there-- he said to my father, he said, why did you remain?
We didn't understand why he meant-- why we remained, we didn't go back with the Russians.
But they left, because the front went fast.
And they left.
And then came in only a few Germans.
We didn't see too many Germans walking the-- we only had one street.
Let's say a street and a half.
So the Germans came in, only a few.
And they organized the worst Ukrainians they could find-- murderers or anything else, just the worst guys they could find-- to make police.
And the first thing the Germans demanded was all the gold and silver, any valuable thing from the Jews to give away.
They came in and took whatever they wanted.
And they roughed it up and everything else.
That was one time.
Then the second time, they said anyone who had any livestock or any living creature in a Jewish home, they cannot have them.
We had a cow, we had chickens, we had ducks, we had all kinds of things.
We had a big barn in the back.
We had a whole underground tunnel which we used to keep potatoes and everything else for the winter times.
And we used to prepare it and save for the winter.
And they-- whatever they want, they just took it away.
They took away the clothes.
My father buried a big box of clothes for my brothers in a hole in the stable.
They found the hole, and they found it.
They took it away.
My father argued with them, but they took it away.
So at that time, we already didn't have even an egg that went from the chicken.
We had to give away something to get from a Ukrainian chicken, or get an egg, or anything like that.
Like this, we used to have our own milk, we had our own stuff.
We still had some things in the fields, but not for very long.
When the winter came in, we were in bad shape.
But every time, they used to come in all the time.
And one time they came in-- it was on a Saturday, I remember-- and they shot my dog.
And they went in-- my father was praying.
What they tell us-- and they said to him that he should go and bury his dog.
And my father said, today is my sabbath, I'm not going to bury the dog.
And if you want, you can bury him yourself.
And he said, you go and bury the dog.
And he said no.
And he hit my father a couple times.
And he said, I'll bury the dog after sundown.
And that's how it happened.
He didn't bury the dog.
He buried the dog in the evening.
At sundown, he buried the dog.
That's one incident.
Another incident, they came in, they looked for something, and they took my father.
Because my father was in the army, he stood up a little bit.
So we had a-- a trout in the back, which we had a-- we had a well.
We didn't have no water-- running water in the house.
We had a well outside.
We used to take it up with a pail.
And beside that, there was a well which they put my father inside.
I ran away, by the way.
I wasn't there.
They put my father in there, and they put some stones and some blocks on top of it.
And I sneaked around the well.
And I listened, and then my father heard and called my name.
And I didn't know where he was.
I thought he was in the well.
OK.
